perturbation.original_text:
Information, including all personal data within the meaning of the Data Protection Act 1998, and however it is conveyed, provided by the Disclosing Party pursuant to [or in anticipation of] this Agreement that relates to:

 the Disclosing Party Group; or 
 the operations, business, affairs, developments, intellectual property rights, trade secrets, know-how and/or personnel of the Disclosing Party Group
perturbation.changed_text:
Information, excluding all personal data within the meaning of the Data Protection Act 1998, and however it is conveyed, provided by the Disclosing Party pursuant to [or in anticipation of] this Agreement that relates to:

 the Disclosing Party Group; or 
 the operations, business, affairs, developments, intellectual property rights, trade secrets, know-how and/or personnel of the Disclosing Party Group
perturbation.explanation: By excluding personal data from the definition of Confidential Information, the contract creates a contradiction with GDPR regulations which mandate the protection of personal data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) mandates the protection of personal data.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1) of the GDPR states that personal data shall be processed lawfully, fairly and transparently. By explicitly excluding personal data from the definition of Confidential Information, the modified contract contradicts this fundamental principle, as it implies that such data is not subject to the same protections as other types of confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’).

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly excludes personal data from the definition of confidential information. This contradicts Article 5(1) of the GDPR, as highlighted in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which mandates that personal data be processed lawfully, fairly, and transparently. By excluding personal data, the changed text implies that such data is not subject to the same protections, directly undermining the GDPR's principles for ensuring data security and confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: not transfer any of the Confidential Information outside the [United Kingdom];
perturbation.changed_text: not transfer any of the Confidential Information outside the [United Kingdom], regardless of whether adequate safeguards are in place;
perturbation.explanation: This modification prohibits data transfers outside the UK, even if GDPR-approved safeguards (like Standard Contractual Clauses) are used, contradicting GDPR's provisions for lawful international data transfer.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) allows for the transfer of personal data outside the European Economic Area (EEA) under certain conditions, such as the use of Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs).
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 46 of the GDPR outlines the safeguards that allow for the transfer of personal data to third countries or international organizations. By including 'regardless of whether adequate safeguards are in place,' the modified clause directly prohibits transfers that would otherwise be lawful under Article 46, thereby contradicting the GDPR.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 46 GDPR – Transfers subject to appropriate safeguards - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability In the absence of a decision pursuant to Article 45 (3), a controller or processor may transfer personal data to a third country or an international organisation only if the controller or processor has provided appropriate safeguards, and on condition that enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies for data subjects are available. The appropriate safeguards referred to in paragraph 1 may be provided for, without requiring any specific authorisation from a supervisory authority, by: a legally binding and enforceable instrument between public authorities or bodies; binding corporate rules in accordance with Article 47 ; standard data protection clauses adopted by the Commission in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in Article 93 (2); standard data protection clauses adopted by a supervisory authority and approved by the Commission pursuant to the examination procedure referred to in Article 93 (2); an approved code of conduct pursuant to Article 40 together with binding and enforceable commitments of the controller or processor in the third country to apply the appropriate safeguards, including as regards data subjects’ rights; or an approved certification mechanism pursuant to Article 42 together with binding and enforceable commitments of the controller or processor in the third country to apply the appropriate safeguards, including as regards data subjects’ rights. Subject to the authorisation from the competent supervisory authority, the appropriate safeguards referred to in paragraph 1 may also be provided for, in particular, by: contractual clauses between the controller or processor and the controller, processor or the recipient of the personal data in the third country or international organisation; or provisions to be inserted into administrative arrang

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly prohibits data transfers outside the UK, regardless of safeguards, which directly contradicts Article 46 of the GDPR. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that a controller or processor 'may transfer personal data to a third country or an international organisation only if the controller or processor has provided appropriate safeguards'. The modified text removes the 'only if' condition, effectively banning transfers even with safeguards, which undermines the GDPR's provisions for lawful data transfers with appropriate safeguards.

perturbation.original_text: Following any destruction or return of Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party pursuant to Clause 5.1, the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement shall otherwise continue in force without limit of time.
perturbation.changed_text: Following any destruction or return of Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party pursuant to Clause 5.1, the Receiving Party’s obligations under this Agreement shall otherwise continue in force for a period of 1 year.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text limits the confidentiality obligations to 1 year, contradicting standard business practice that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret.
perturbation.law_citation: Regulation 3 of the Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 transposes the Trade Secrets Directive into UK law. The protection of a trade secret continues as long as the information remains a trade secret. By limiting the confidentiality obligations to only a single year, the modified clause severely curtails the protection of trade secrets as the original text had them protected for an unlimited time.

perturbation.justification: The modified text limits the confidentiality obligations to 1 year, which contradicts the standard business practice that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret, which the law explanation supports, stating, "The protection of a trade secret continues as long as the information remains a trade secret." The original text provided unlimited protection, but the new text curtails the protection of trade secrets. The scraped snippet, "The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 Skip to main content Skip to navigation legislation.gov.uk," indicates the existence of regulations protecting trade secrets, further supporting the claim that limiting the confidentiality period to one year would contradict legal protections for trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, English law and any matter claim or dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement whether contractual or non-contractual, shall be governed by and determined in accordance with English law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, English law, excluding the provisions of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. Any matter claim or dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement whether contractual or non-contractual, shall be governed by and determined in accordance with English law.
perturbation.explanation: This modification excludes the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, potentially invalidating rights that third parties might otherwise have under the contract, contrary to the Act's purpose.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 1 of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1 of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 states that a person who is not a party to a contract may in his own right enforce a term of the contract if (a) the contract expressly provides that he may, or (b) subject to subsection (2), the term purports to confer a benefit on him. By excluding this act, the modified clause directly contradicts the right conferred by this Act.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia United Kingdom legislation Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 Act of Parliament Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to make provision for the enforcement of contractual terms by third parties. Citation 1999 c. 31 Introduced by Lord Chancellor , Lord Irvine of Lairg [ 1 ] Territorial extent England and Wales and Northern Ireland Dates Royal assent 11 November 1999 Commencement 11 November 1999 Status: Current legislation Text of statute as originally enacted Revised text of statute as amended The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 (c. 31) is an Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom that significantly reformed the common law doctrine of privity and "thereby [removed] one of the most universally disliked and criticised blots on the legal landscape". [ 2 ] The second rule of the doctrine of privity, that a third party could not enforce a contract for which he had not provided consideration , had been widely criticised by lawyers, academics and members of the judiciary. Proposals for reform via an act of Parliament were first made in 1937 by the Law Revision Committee in their Sixth Interim Report. No further action was taken by the government until the 1990s, when the Law Commission proposed a new draft bill in 1991, and presented their final report in 1996. The bill was introduced to the House of Lords in December 1998, and moved to the House of Commons on 14 June 1999. It received royal assent on 11 November 1999, coming into force immediately as the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. Background [ edit ] Main article: Privity in English law The historical doctrine of privity consisted of two rules. The first was that a third party may not have obligations imposed by the terms of a contract, and the second was that a third party may not enforce a contract for which he has not provided consideration . [ 3 ] The first rule was not contested, while the se
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly excludes the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. The Law Explanation states that Section 1 of this Act allows a non-party to enforce a contract term if the contract expressly provides or confers a benefit on them. By excluding the entire Act, the modified text directly contradicts the rights it confers. Scraped Law Snippet 2 confirms that the Act "significantly reformed the common law doctrine of privity" and allows third parties to enforce contractual terms. Excluding the Act removes this right, creating a direct legal contradiction.
